reasoning: The finding at line 220 of class-bsr-db.php ('SELECT * FROM `$table` LIMIT $start, $end') is NOT an exploitable vulnerability. Full path analysis: (1) Access gate: the only reachable callsite is BSR_AJAX::process_search_replace(), which requires both a valid nonce (check_admin_referer) and manage_options capability (bsr_enabled_for_user()). No lower-privilege path to srdb() exists. (2) $table guard: before reaching srdb(), the table name must survive table_exists(), which checks in_array($table, get_tables()) — it must be a real table already in the database. Inside srdb(), $table = esc_sql($table) is applied before interpolation inside backticks. esc_sql() escapes backtick characters (\`), preventing identifier escape. A crafted table name cannot break out of the backtick context. (3) $start guard: computed as $page * $this->page_size where $page comes from absint($_REQUEST['bsr_page']) in the AJAX handler and $this->page_size is absint(get_option('bsr_page_size')). The product of two non-negative integers is always an integer — no injection surface. (4) $end guard: equals $this->page_size directly, always an integer via absint(). Conclusion: all three interpolated variables have solid, independently-verified integer or identifier guards. The absence of $wpdb->prepare() is an anti-pattern and a maintenance risk (future modifications could inadvertently drop a guard), but the current code has no practical SQL injection path. Confirmed as is_real=false.
